title: A good story is hard to change
A good explanation is hard to change
A good explanation is hard to vary
A good explanation resists variation
Good explanations resist variation
deutsch-principle: A scientist named David Deutsch said something simple and useful: a good explanation is hard to change. Every piece of it is doing a real job. So if you swap out one little detail, the whole thing falls apart. A bad explanation is the opposite. It sounds smooth and believable, but it would fit almost anything that could happen. Think about saying "sales went up because the market changed." That explains why sales went up. But you could say the exact same words if sales had gone down instead. It doesn't really pin anything down. And because it doesn't pin anything down, it can't tell you what will happen next.

The physicist David Deutsch said it well: a good explanation is hard to change. Every part of it is doing real work, so you can't swap a detail without the whole thing breaking. A bad explanation is the opposite. It sounds smooth and believable, and it would fit any result just as easily. "Sales rose because the market shifted" explains a rise — but you could tell the same story about a fall. It commits to nothing, so it predicts nothing.

The physicist David Deutsch put it sharply: a good explanation is hard to vary. Every part does load-bearing work, so you can't change a detail without wrecking the account. A bad explanation is the opposite — it's plausible, fluent, and would fit any outcome just as comfortably. "Sales rose because the market shifted" explains a rise, and you could tell the identical story about a fall. It commits to nothing, so it predicts nothing.

Deutsch put it sharply: a good explanation is hard to vary. Every part is load-bearing; change a detail and the account breaks. A bad explanation is the inverse — plausible, fluent, fitting any outcome equally. "Sales rose because the market shifted" explains a rise and an identical-sounding fall. Committing to nothing, it predicts nothing.

Deutsch: good explanations are hard to vary — every part load-bearing, no detail swappable. Bad ones are plausible and fit any outcome ("sales rose because the market shifted" fits a fall too). Commit to nothing, predict nothing.
ai-prose-defence: This is exactly the kind of writing an AI is really good at: smooth, confident words that can be wrapped around anything that already happened. So how do you defend against it? You ask for the parts that could turn out to be wrong — the real numbers, the how-it-works details, the things one piece depends on — and then you go check them. That's why it helps to Show the data; don't just assert it, because the proof is the very thing a too-easy story quietly leaves out. It's also why Structure beats freeform text for working with AI: a clear shape forces those promises out into the open instead of letting them hide inside the flow of the words.

This is exactly the kind of text an AI is good at making: confident, after-the-fact prose that drapes over whatever happened. The defence is to ask for the parts that could be wrong — the specific numbers, mechanisms, and dependencies — and then check them. That's why Show the data; don't just assert it, because the evidence is what an easy-to-vary story quietly skips. And it's why Structure beats freeform text for working with AI: a schema forces the commitments into the open instead of letting them hide in flow.

This is exactly the kind of text an AI is good at producing: confident, post-hoc prose that drapes over whatever happened. The defence is to demand the parts that could be wrong — the specific numbers, mechanisms, and dependencies — and then check them. That's where Show the data; don't just assert it, because the evidence is what an easy-to-vary story quietly skips. And it's why Structure beats freeform text for working with AI: a schema forces the commitments into the open instead of letting them hide in flow.

AI excels at exactly this: confident, post-hoc prose draped over any outcome. The defence — demand the falsifiable parts (numbers, mechanisms, dependencies) and check them. Hence Show the data; don't just assert it: evidence is what an easy-to-vary story skips. And Structure beats freeform text for working with AI — a schema forces commitments into the open.

AI's specialty: post-hoc prose fitting any outcome. Defence — demand falsifiable parts (numbers, mechanisms, dependencies); check them. Hence Show the data; don't just assert it; hence Structure beats freeform text for working with AI — schema surfaces commitments.
cant-be-wrong: Here's the heart of it. If a claim can never be shown to be wrong, then you can't really trust it either. That's why it helps to think of Trust is a calibrated forecast, not a feeling as a careful guess about what will happen, instead of just a warm feeling. To go deeper, see the frameworks for explanation.

A claim that can never be wrong can't really be trusted either. That's the whole point of treating Trust is a calibrated forecast, not a feeling as a careful forecast rather than a feeling. See the frameworks for explanation.
A claim that can't be wrong can't be trusted either, which is the whole point of treating Trust is a calibrated forecast, not a feeling rather than a feeling. See the frameworks for explanation.
A claim that can't be wrong can't be trusted — the point of treating Trust is a calibrated forecast, not a feeling as forecast, not feeling. See the frameworks for explanation.
Unfalsifiable means untrustworthy — hence Trust is a calibrated forecast, not a feeling. See the frameworks for explanation.
